name: Mortgage Payment Calculator
description: Calculate monthly mortgage payments for   US & UK real estate.
what_is: Buying a home is a massive milestone that requires a clear view of your future monthly costs. This tool calculates your estimated monthly mortgage payment by factoring in the home price, your down payment, the loan term, and the current interest rate.
who_use: If you are a first-time homebuyer trying to see what you can afford, or a real estate investor looking to flip a property in the US or UK, this is for you.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total purchase price. 2. Input the interest rate. 3. Select the loan term. 4. Hit calculate.
how_to_read: The result shows you the fixed monthly amount you will pay to your lender. Focus on how this fits into your take-home pay to ensure you aren't 'house poor'.
pro_tip: Try to keep your total mortgage payment under 28% of your gross monthly income to maintain a healthy financial cushion. Use our tool to test how a larger down payment can significantly lower your monthly bill!

name: Auto Loan EMI Calculator
description: Estimate monthly installment payments for new or used cars.
what_is: Getting behind the wheel of a new car is exciting, but the monthly bill should never come as a surprise. This calculator estimates your Equated Monthly Installment (EMI) for an auto loan, whether you are buying from a private seller or a dealership.
who_use: This tool is perfect for car buyers financing through a bank, credit union, or dealership. It is also highly useful for existing car owners considering refinancing their current loan.
how_to_use: 1. Type in the total loan amount. 2. Enter the annual interest rate. 3. Select the loan duration in months. 4. Click calculate.
how_to_read: Your result displays the exact amount you need to set aside every month. Look closely at the total interest figure — this tells you how much extra you are paying for the convenience of financing.
pro_tip: Aim for a loan term of 48 months or less to avoid paying more in interest than the car is worth. Use this calculator to see how much you save by shortening your loan by just 12 months!

name: Personal Loan Calculator
description: Calculate monthly installments for personal credit lines.
what_is: A personal loan can cover anything from a medical emergency to a home renovation. This calculator breaks down your loan into clear monthly installments so you can borrow with confidence and repay without stress.
who_use: Anyone taking an unsecured personal loan from a bank, credit union, or online lender will benefit from this tool. It is especially helpful if you are comparing offers from multiple lenders.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the amount you plan to borrow. 2. Input the lender's annual interest rate. 3. Choose your repayment period in months. 4. Hit calculate to see your monthly payment.
how_to_read: The result shows your fixed monthly repayment. Multiply this by the number of months to see your total repayment — the difference from your loan amount is the total interest cost.
pro_tip: Before signing any loan agreement, use this calculator to compare at least three lenders. Even a 1% difference in interest rate can save you hundreds of dollars over the life of the loan!

name: SBA 7(a) Business Loan Calculator
description: Specialized calculator for US Small Business Administration loans.
what_is: The SBA 7(a) loan is the US government's most popular small business financing program, offering longer terms and lower rates than conventional loans. This calculator helps you estimate your monthly repayment before you walk into a lender's office.
who_use: US small business owners applying for SBA-backed financing — whether for working capital, equipment, or real estate — will find this tool invaluable for budgeting and planning.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total requested loan amount. 2. Input the SBA-negotiated interest rate. 3. Set the loan term in months (SBA 7(a) goes up to 300 months for real estate). 4. Click calculate.
how_to_read: Your monthly payment result helps you assess whether the business cash flow can comfortably cover the obligation. A healthy business should cover this payment at least 1.25 times with operating income.
pro_tip: SBA 7(a) rates are typically Prime Rate + a small markup. Check the current Prime Rate before entering your number for the most accurate estimate. Start your calculation here!

name: Student Loan Repayment Calculator
description: Plan your student debt payoff strategy.
what_is: Student debt can feel overwhelming, but a clear repayment plan makes it manageable. This calculator shows you exactly what your monthly payment will be and how long it will take to become completely debt-free based on your loan balance and interest rate.
who_use: This tool is built for recent graduates, current students with existing debt, and parents who co-signed education loans. It is also useful if you are considering refinancing to a lower rate.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your current total loan balance. 2. Input your interest rate. 3. Choose your desired repayment term. 4. Calculate to see your monthly commitment.
how_to_read: Your result shows the monthly payment required to clear the debt within your chosen timeline. The shorter the term you select, the higher the payment — but the less total interest you pay overall.
pro_tip: Even paying $50 extra per month on your student loans can cut years off your repayment timeline. Try adjusting the term in this calculator to see exactly how much sooner you could be debt-free!

name: Credit Card Payoff Calculator
description: Plan your journey to becoming debt-free.
what_is: Credit card debt is one of the most expensive forms of borrowing, with interest rates often exceeding 20% APR. This calculator shows you precisely how long it will take to pay off your balance and how much interest you will pay in total.
who_use: Anyone carrying a balance on one or more credit cards month to month needs this tool. It is especially powerful for people who have been making only minimum payments without seeing the balance drop.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your current card balance. 2. Input your card's annual interest rate. 3. Enter the monthly payment you can afford. 4. Click calculate to see your payoff timeline.
how_to_read: The result reveals how many months until you are debt-free and the total interest paid. If that number shocks you, increase your monthly payment in the tool to find a timeline that motivates you.
pro_tip: Paying just double the minimum payment can cut your payoff time in half on most cards. Use this calculator to find your personal tipping point and start your debt-free countdown today!

name: Equipment Lease Calculator
description: Calculate monthly lease costs for business machinery and tools.
what_is: Leasing equipment lets your business use expensive machinery without a large upfront purchase. This calculator estimates your monthly lease payment so you can decide whether leasing or buying makes more financial sense for your specific situation.
who_use: Small business owners, contractors, and startup founders who need heavy equipment, vehicles, or technology but want to preserve cash flow will get the most value from this tool.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total market value of the equipment. 2. Input the lease interest rate offered by your provider. 3. Set the lease duration in months. 4. Hit calculate.
how_to_read: Your result is the fixed monthly lease payment. Compare this to the cost of purchasing outright — factor in depreciation and maintenance to get the full picture of which option is cheaper long-term.
pro_tip: Before signing a lease, always check the residual value clause. A lower residual value means higher monthly payments but a cheaper buyout at the end. Use this calculator to model both scenarios!

name: Debt-to-Income (DTI) Ratio Calculator
description: Check your loan eligibility with DTI ratio.
what_is: Your Debt-to-Income ratio is the single most important number lenders look at when deciding whether to approve your loan application. This calculator instantly computes your DTI so you know exactly where you stand before applying.
who_use: Anyone planning to apply for a mortgage, car loan, or personal loan should check their DTI first. It is also valuable for people who want to understand why a previous loan application was declined.
how_to_use: 1. Add up all your monthly debt payments (rent, cards, existing loans). 2. Enter your total gross monthly income before taxes. 3. Click calculate to get your DTI percentage.
how_to_read: A DTI below 36% is considered healthy by most lenders. Between 36–43% is borderline. Above 43% means most mortgage lenders will reject your application until you reduce debt or increase income.
pro_tip: If your DTI is too high, paying off a small loan entirely — even a low-balance credit card — can move the needle significantly. Calculate your new DTI after each debt payoff to track your progress!

name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) Calculator
description: Essential for commercial loan approval analysis.
what_is: The DSCR is the benchmark commercial lenders use to decide if your business or property generates enough income to cover its debt obligations. This calculator gives you your DSCR instantly so you can assess your loan readiness before approaching a bank.
who_use: Real estate investors, property developers, and business owners seeking large commercial loans need to know their DSCR before meeting with lenders. It is a non-negotiable metric in commercial lending.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your property or business net operating income (NOI). 2. Input your total annual debt service (principal + interest payments). 3. Click calculate for your DSCR score.
how_to_read: A DSCR of 1.25 or higher is the typical minimum lenders require. A score of 1.0 means income exactly covers debt — no cushion. Below 1.0 means you are operating at a loss relative to debt.
pro_tip: If your DSCR is below 1.25, focus on increasing NOI by reducing vacancies or operational expenses before applying. Run this calculator monthly to track your progress toward lender-ready numbers!

name: Loan Amortization Schedule Calculator
description: See a full breakdown of principal vs. interest for every payment.
what_is: An amortization schedule is a complete table showing exactly how each monthly loan payment is split between paying down your principal balance and paying interest to the lender. This calculator generates that full picture so you understand where every dollar goes.
who_use: Homebuyers, business loan recipients, and anyone with a large installment loan will find this tool eye-opening. It is especially useful for people who want to make extra payments and see the impact.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total loan amount. 2. Input the annual interest rate. 3. Set the loan term in months. 4. Click calculate to generate the full schedule.
how_to_read: In the early months, most of your payment goes to interest. As time passes, more goes toward principal. Look at the cumulative interest column — that is the true cost of your loan beyond the principal.
pro_tip: Making even one extra principal payment per year can shave years off your loan and save thousands in interest. Use this schedule to find the exact month where extra payments have the biggest impact!

name: Marketing ROI Calculator
description: Measure the return on your ad spend and campaigns.
what_is: Marketing ROI tells you whether your advertising spend is actually making you money or quietly draining your budget. This calculator measures the return on every dollar you invest in campaigns, ads, and promotions so you can spend smarter.
who_use: Digital marketers, business owners running paid ads on Google or Meta, and marketing managers who need to justify their budget to stakeholders will find this tool essential for every campaign review.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total revenue directly attributed to your campaign. 2. Input the total cost of that marketing effort. 3. Click calculate to instantly see your ROI percentage.
how_to_read: A positive ROI means your campaign made more money than it cost. An ROI of 100% means you doubled your investment. If your ROI is negative, the campaign lost money and needs to be restructured.
pro_tip: Track ROI per channel, not just overall. A single high-performing channel can mask several losing ones. Use this calculator after every campaign to build a data-driven marketing budget!

name: Rental Property ROI Calculator
description: Calculate annual return on investment for rental properties.
what_is: Owning a rental property is only a good investment if the numbers actually work. This calculator measures your annual return on investment by comparing your net rental income to the total money you have tied up in the property.
who_use: Landlords evaluating an existing property, real estate investors comparing multiple deals, and first-time property buyers deciding whether to rent or sell will all benefit from running these numbers.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total annual rental income. 2. Input all annual expenses including mortgage, taxes, insurance, and maintenance. 3. Enter the current property value. 4. Calculate your ROI.
how_to_read: A rental ROI above 8% is generally considered strong in most markets. Below 4% means your money might work harder elsewhere. Use the result to benchmark against other investment options.
pro_tip: Do not forget to include vacancy rate in your expenses — most properties sit empty for 4–8 weeks per year. Factor this in from the start so your ROI calculation reflects real-world performance!

name: Stock Profit Calculator
description: Calculate your capital gains from stock market trading.
what_is: Whether you are a day trader or a long-term investor, knowing your exact profit or loss on a stock position is fundamental. This calculator computes your capital gain or loss and return percentage so you always know exactly where you stand.
who_use: Retail investors, active traders, and anyone who buys and sells individual stocks will use this constantly. It is also useful at tax time when you need to calculate realized gains for reporting.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the price you paid per share (buy price). 2. Input the price at which you sold (sell price). 3. Enter the number of shares traded. 4. Click calculate for your total profit or loss.
how_to_read: A positive result means you made a profit — that is a capital gain. A negative result is a capital loss. Your return percentage tells you how efficiently your money worked compared to its starting value.
pro_tip: Always factor in brokerage commissions and taxes when evaluating a trade. A 5% gain on paper can shrink significantly after fees. Include those costs in your buy/sell price for a true net profit figure!

name: Crypto Gains Calculator
description: Track your profits and losses for BTC, ETH, and altcoins.
what_is: Cryptocurrency markets move fast and your profit picture can change in minutes. This calculator gives you an instant, accurate view of your gains or losses on any crypto position — from Bitcoin to the smallest altcoin — so you can make informed decisions.
who_use: Crypto traders looking to lock in profits, HODLers checking their portfolio performance, and tax filers who need to report digital asset gains will all get clear, actionable numbers from this tool.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total amount you originally invested in USD. 2. Input the current or exit value of your position. 3. Click calculate to see your profit, loss, and return percentage.
how_to_read: A positive figure means your crypto position gained value — your investment grew. A negative figure is a loss. The percentage return tells you how your crypto performed relative to a traditional investment.
pro_tip: In most countries, crypto gains are taxable events. Keep a record of every buy and sell price with this calculator and consult a tax advisor to avoid surprises at the end of the financial year!

name: Dividend Yield Calculator
description: Calculate annual dividend returns from stocks.
what_is: Dividend investing is one of the most reliable ways to generate passive income from the stock market. This calculator shows you your annual dividend yield percentage and total yearly income so you can compare dividend stocks and build a reliable income portfolio.
who_use: Income investors building a dividend portfolio, retirees seeking passive cash flow, and anyone evaluating whether a stock's dividend payment justifies its current price should run this calculation before every purchase.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the annual dividend paid per share. 2. Input the current stock price. 3. Add the number of shares you own or plan to buy. 4. Calculate to see your yield and total annual income.
how_to_read: A yield between 2–5% is generally considered healthy and sustainable. Above 8% can signal risk — the company may be struggling or the dividend could be cut. Always research the payout ratio alongside the yield.
pro_tip: Reinvesting your dividends automatically (DRIP) can dramatically accelerate your portfolio growth through compounding. Use this calculator to project how your income grows as you reinvest year after year!

name: Cap Rate Calculator
description: Evaluate commercial real estate investment returns.
what_is: The capitalization rate — or cap rate — is the most widely used metric for evaluating commercial real estate investments. It measures the annual return a property generates relative to its market value, independent of financing, making it a pure measure of asset performance.
who_use: Commercial property investors, real estate analysts, and buyers comparing multiple properties across different markets use cap rate as a quick, standardized filter to identify the strongest investment opportunities.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the property's annual net operating income (gross rent minus operating expenses). 2. Input the current market value or purchase price of the property. 3. Click calculate.
how_to_read: A cap rate between 5–10% is typical for most commercial markets. A higher cap rate suggests higher returns but also higher risk. Lower cap rates indicate stable, lower-risk properties in premium locations.
pro_tip: Never compare cap rates across different cities without context — a 4% cap rate in Manhattan and a 4% cap rate in rural Ohio represent very different risk profiles. Always benchmark against local market averages!

name: Compound Interest Calculator
description: See how your savings grow with monthly compounding.
what_is: Compound interest is the most powerful force in personal finance — it is the process of earning interest on both your original savings and the interest already accumulated. This calculator shows you exactly how your money multiplies over time with monthly compounding.
who_use: Anyone with a savings account, fixed deposit, or investment portfolio should use this tool regularly. It is especially eye-opening for young savers who want to understand the long-term value of starting early.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your starting principal (how much you have now). 2. Input the annual interest rate. 3. Set the number of years you plan to save. 4. Click calculate to see your future balance.
how_to_read: The result shows your total future balance and how much of it is pure interest earned. The gap between your principal and final balance is the power of compounding — money working for you without extra effort.
pro_tip: Starting just 5 years earlier can add tens of thousands of dollars to your final balance. Use this calculator to compare starting now vs. starting in 5 years — the difference will motivate you to act today!

name: Savings Goal Calculator
description: Find out how much to save monthly to reach your financial target.
what_is: Whether you are saving for a vacation, emergency fund, wedding, or down payment, you need to know exactly how much to set aside each month. This calculator reverse-engineers your savings plan — you tell it the goal, and it tells you the monthly number to hit it.
who_use: Anyone with a specific financial target and a deadline will benefit from this tool. It is ideal for short-term goals like holidays and medium-term goals like home down payments or college funds.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total savings target. 2. Input the interest rate your savings will earn. 3. Set how many months you have to reach the goal. 4. Calculate to get your required monthly savings amount.
how_to_read: The result is your required monthly deposit. If this number is higher than you can comfortably save, either extend your timeline or reduce your goal. Small adjustments can make a big difference.
pro_tip: Automate your monthly savings transfer the day after your paycheck arrives. Treating your savings goal as a non-negotiable bill — not an afterthought — is the single biggest predictor of success. Calculate your number and set it up today!

name: Retirement Fund Goal Calculator
description: Calculate how much you need for a comfortable retirement.
what_is: Retirement planning is not about guessing — it is about knowing your number. This calculator estimates the total nest egg you need to sustain your desired monthly lifestyle throughout retirement, factoring in investment returns to give you a realistic, actionable target.
who_use: Working adults at any age who want to understand how much they need to save for retirement will find this tool essential. It is also valuable for people who have started late and want to understand what is still achievable.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your estimated monthly spending in retirement. 2. Input the number of years you expect to be retired. 3. Add the expected annual return on your retirement portfolio. 4. Calculate your total required nest egg.
how_to_read: The result is the total retirement fund you need to accumulate. This is your target number. Work backward to determine how much you need to save each month between now and your retirement date.
pro_tip: The 4% rule suggests withdrawing no more than 4% of your portfolio annually in retirement. Your result from this calculator reflects that principle. Plug your number into our Savings Goal Calculator to build your monthly savings plan!

name: Business Valuation Calculator
description: Estimate the market value of your small business.
what_is: Whether you are planning to sell your business, bring in investors, or simply want to know what you have built, understanding your company's market value is essential. This calculator uses the earnings multiple method — the most common small business valuation approach — to give you a fast, reliable estimate.
who_use: Small business owners preparing for a sale, entrepreneurs seeking investment, business buyers performing due diligence, and partners negotiating equity splits all need a clear valuation baseline.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your business's annual net profit. 2. Input the standard industry multiple for your sector (typically 2–5x for small businesses). 3. Click calculate to get your estimated valuation.
how_to_read: Your result is the estimated fair market value of your business. This is a starting point — actual sale prices are affected by assets, growth trajectory, customer concentration, and market conditions.
pro_tip: Different industries carry very different multiples. A SaaS business might sell for 8–12x profit while a local restaurant might fetch only 2–3x. Research your specific industry multiple before entering it here for the most accurate result!

name: Earnings Per Share (EPS) Calculator
description: Calculate earnings per share for stock analysis.
what_is: Earnings Per Share is one of the most fundamental metrics in stock analysis, showing how much profit a company generates for each share of stock. This calculator computes EPS instantly so you can compare companies, evaluate growth, and make more informed investment decisions.
who_use: Individual stock investors, portfolio managers, financial analysts, and students studying fundamental analysis will use EPS constantly to screen and evaluate publicly traded companies.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the company's total net income. 2. Subtract any preferred dividends paid. 3. Input the total number of shares outstanding. 4. Calculate to get EPS in dollars per share.
how_to_read: A higher EPS generally indicates greater profitability. More importantly, track EPS growth over multiple quarters — consistently rising EPS is one of the strongest signals of a healthy, growing company.
pro_tip: Compare EPS against the stock's current price to calculate the P/E ratio — divide price by EPS. A lower P/E relative to industry peers can signal an undervalued stock. Use this calculator as your first step in fundamental stock screening!

name: Return on Equity (ROE) Calculator
description: Measure how effectively management uses shareholder equity.
what_is: Return on Equity measures how much profit a company generates with the money shareholders have invested. It is a direct report card on management's ability to grow the business efficiently. This calculator gives you the ROE percentage in seconds.
who_use: Investors comparing companies within the same industry, analysts conducting fundamental research, and business owners benchmarking their own efficiency against competitors all rely on ROE as a core performance metric.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the company's annual net income. 2. Input total shareholder equity from the balance sheet. 3. Click calculate to get the ROE percentage.
how_to_read: An ROE above 15% is generally considered strong. Warren Buffett typically targets companies with ROE consistently above 20%. Low or declining ROE can signal inefficient management or heavy debt load.
pro_tip: Always check if high ROE is driven by genuine profitability or by heavy debt — borrowing artificially inflates ROE. Pair this calculation with a debt-to-equity check for a complete picture of company health!

name: Shopify Profit Margin Calculator
description: Calculate your store's gross profit margin.
what_is: Running a Shopify store means managing product costs, platform fees, and shipping all at once. This calculator strips away all those costs to show your true profit margin — the percentage of each sale that actually lands in your pocket after expenses.
who_use: Shopify store owners at every stage — from those just launching their first product to seasoned merchants managing hundreds of SKUs — need to know their real margin to price products correctly and grow sustainably.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total revenue for the period. 2. Input your cost of goods sold. 3. Add Shopify subscription and transaction fees. 4. Calculate to see your net profit margin percentage.
how_to_read: A margin above 20% is generally healthy for e-commerce. If your margin is in single digits, your pricing or cost structure needs attention. Use the result to identify which products are worth scaling and which are draining profit.
pro_tip: Shopify's transaction fees vary by plan — upgrading to a higher plan can actually increase total profit if your volume is high enough. Use this calculator to model the break-even point between plan costs and fee savings!

name: Amazon FBA Fee Margin Calculator
description: Estimate profit after Amazon FBA and referral fees.
what_is: Amazon FBA sellers face a complex stack of fees — referral fees, fulfillment fees, storage fees — that can quietly erode your margins. This calculator consolidates all those costs and shows you the real profit you keep from every unit sold on Amazon.
who_use: Current Amazon FBA sellers evaluating product profitability, new sellers vetting potential products before sourcing, and private label brands scaling their catalog all need accurate margin data before making inventory decisions.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your product's selling price. 2. Input your total product cost including sourcing and shipping to Amazon. 3. Add your estimated FBA fulfillment and referral fees. 4. Calculate your net profit per unit.
how_to_read: Your result shows net profit per unit and margin percentage. Aim for at least a 25–30% margin after all Amazon fees. Anything below 15% is difficult to scale profitably once advertising costs are factored in.
pro_tip: Amazon's referral fee varies by category — from 6% for electronics to 17% for jewelry. Always use your actual category rate, not a generic average, for an accurate calculation. Run this before ordering any new inventory!

name: eBay Profit Calculator
description: Calculate net profit after eBay and payment fees.
what_is: eBay selling involves a layered fee structure including final value fees, payment processing charges, and optional listing upgrades. This calculator cuts through the complexity to show you the net profit you actually keep from each sale.
who_use: Casual eBay sellers flipping items, professional resellers running a high-volume eBay business, and dropshippers listing supplier products on eBay all need to verify profitability before — not after — a sale.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the price you sold the item for. 2. Input your total product acquisition cost. 3. Add up all eBay and payment processing fees. 4. Click calculate to see your real take-home profit.
how_to_read: If your profit number is smaller than expected, your fees are likely eating more than you realized. A healthy eBay margin sits above 20% — anything lower makes scaling difficult when shipping issues or returns arise.
pro_tip: eBay's final value fee is typically 13.25% plus a fixed fee per order. Factor in PayPal or managed payments fees separately. Use this calculator for every new product category you add to ensure each one pulls its weight!

name: Gross Profit Margin Calculator
description: Calculate the percentage of revenue above cost of goods sold.
what_is: Gross profit margin tells you how much of your sales revenue remains after subtracting the direct cost of producing or purchasing your products. It is the foundational health metric for any product-based business and the starting point for all profitability analysis.
who_use: Product business owners, retail store managers, wholesalers, manufacturers, and e-commerce entrepreneurs use gross margin to benchmark pricing, evaluate suppliers, and compare performance across their product line.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total revenue for the period. 2. Input your total cost of goods sold (COGS) — the direct cost to produce or source your products. 3. Click calculate to see your gross margin percentage.
how_to_read: A higher gross margin means more money remains to cover operating expenses and generate profit. Industry benchmarks vary widely — software companies often exceed 70%, while grocery retail may operate at 25%.
pro_tip: If your gross margin is shrinking quarter over quarter, your supplier costs are rising faster than your prices. This calculator helps you spot that trend early — before it becomes a cash flow crisis. Run it monthly!

name: Net Profit Margin Calculator
description: Find your actual profit after all business expenses.
what_is: While gross margin shows profit before overhead, net profit margin shows what you actually keep after every single expense — rent, salaries, marketing, taxes, and everything else. It is the truest measure of your business's real-world financial health.
who_use: Business owners seeking a complete picture of profitability, investors evaluating a company before acquiring it, and accountants preparing financial reports all rely on net margin as the bottom-line performance metric.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total revenue for the period. 2. Input the sum of all business expenses including COGS, operating costs, interest, and taxes. 3. Calculate your net profit margin percentage.
how_to_read: A net margin of 10% is generally considered healthy across most industries. Under 5% is thin and vulnerable to disruption. Above 20% is exceptional. Track this number monthly to catch negative trends early.
pro_tip: The difference between your gross margin and net margin reveals your overhead burden. If the gap is large, your fixed costs are high — a risk in slow revenue months. Use both calculators together for a complete financial health check!

name: Price Markup Calculator
description: Determine the markup needed to reach your profit goal.
what_is: Pricing a product incorrectly is one of the most common and costly mistakes in business. This calculator determines the exact selling price and markup percentage you need to achieve your desired profit margin — removing guesswork from one of your most critical business decisions.
who_use: Retailers, wholesalers, manufacturers, freelancers setting service rates, and any business owner who needs to price products or services to hit specific profit targets will use this tool constantly.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the cost price of your product or service. 2. Input your desired profit margin percentage. 3. Click calculate to get the selling price and markup percentage you need to charge.
how_to_read: Note that markup and margin are different things. A 50% markup means you added 50% to the cost, but the margin is only 33%. Your result clearly shows both so you can communicate accurately with buyers and your accounting team.
pro_tip: Always price based on margin, not markup — margin accounts for a percentage of the final selling price, which aligns with how profit is actually measured. Use this calculator to eliminate pricing errors that silently kill your profitability!

name: Break-Even Sales Volume Calculator
description: Find how many units you must sell to cover all costs.
what_is: The break-even point is the minimum sales volume your business needs to avoid a loss. Below this number you are losing money; above it every unit sold generates profit. This calculator tells you exactly how many units you need to sell to break even.
who_use: Entrepreneurs launching a new product, small business owners evaluating a new service line, and anyone building a financial model needs to know their break-even point before committing capital.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total fixed costs (rent, salaries, subscriptions). 2. Input the variable cost per unit (materials, packaging, shipping). 3. Enter your selling price per unit. 4. Calculate your break-even quantity.
how_to_read: If your break-even number feels unrealistically high given your market size, your pricing is too low or your fixed costs are too high. Adjust the inputs to find the pricing and cost structure that makes the business viable.
pro_tip: Calculate break-even in both units and revenue — knowing you need to sell 500 units AND generate $25,000 to break even gives you a clearer sales target. Use this before launching any new product or entering a new market!

name: Startup Burn Rate Calculator
description: Calculate how long your cash reserves will last.
what_is: Burn rate is the speed at which a startup spends its cash reserves before becoming profitable. Knowing your runway — how many months of cash you have left — is the most critical piece of information for any early-stage founder navigating toward profitability.
who_use: Startup founders managing investor capital, pre-revenue businesses tracking survival timeline, and early-stage teams making hiring and spending decisions all need to monitor burn rate and runway constantly.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your current total cash balance. 2. Input your average monthly operating expenses. 3. Enter any monthly revenue you are already generating. 4. Calculate your net burn and runway in months.
how_to_read: Your result shows your net monthly burn (expenses minus revenue) and how many months of runway you have at the current rate. If that number is under 6 months, it is time to either cut costs or accelerate fundraising.
pro_tip: Always plan your fundraise or profitability push to complete at least 3 months before your runway expires. Investors and lenders can smell desperation — entering a raise with 12+ months of runway gives you negotiating power. Calculate yours now!

name: Inventory Turnover Ratio Calculator
description: Measure efficiency in managing business inventory.
what_is: Inventory turnover measures how many times your business sells through its entire stock in a given period. A high turnover means your products are moving fast and cash is not trapped in unsold goods. This calculator reveals how efficiently you are managing your inventory.
who_use: Retail store owners, warehouse managers, e-commerce sellers, and product-based businesses of all sizes need to track inventory turnover to minimize carrying costs and maximize cash flow.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your cost of goods sold for the period. 2. Input the average inventory value (beginning inventory + ending inventory divided by 2). 3. Click calculate to get your turnover ratio.
how_to_read: Most retail businesses aim for a turnover of 4–6 times per year. A ratio below 2 means stock is sitting too long and tying up cash. A very high ratio could mean you are selling out too quickly and missing sales.
pro_tip: Track turnover by product category, not just overall. You may discover that 20% of your products account for 80% of your turnover — and the rest are dead stock draining storage costs. Use this insight to rationalize your catalog!

name: Working Capital Ratio Calculator
description: Check your business liquidity and financial health.
what_is: Working capital ratio — also known as the current ratio — measures your business's ability to pay its short-term obligations with its short-term assets. It is the essential liquidity check that tells you whether your business can survive unexpected cash demands.
who_use: Small business owners assessing financial health, accountants preparing for lender meetings, and investors evaluating a company's short-term stability all rely on this ratio as a first-pass viability test.
how_to_use: 1. Add up all current assets (cash, accounts receivable, inventory). 2. Add up all current liabilities (accounts payable, short-term loans, taxes due). 3. Click calculate to get your ratio.
how_to_read: A ratio above 1.5 means your business can comfortably meet short-term obligations. Below 1.0 means you owe more than you can currently pay — a serious warning sign. Between 1.0 and 1.5 is acceptable but tight.
pro_tip: Improving your working capital ratio is often a matter of collecting receivables faster or negotiating longer payment terms with suppliers. Even a small improvement can shift you from financially stressed to financially stable. Track this monthly!

name: USA Sales Tax Calculator
description: Quickly add the correct sales tax to your product prices.
what_is: Sales tax in the United States varies by state, county, and sometimes city — making it one of the most confusing pricing elements for businesses and shoppers alike. This calculator instantly adds the correct sales tax to any product price so you always charge and collect the right amount.
who_use: US small business owners selling in-person or online, e-commerce sellers with nexus in multiple states, and shoppers wanting to know the final cost of a purchase before checkout will all use this tool regularly.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the base price of your product or service. 2. Input your applicable state (or combined state + local) sales tax rate. 3. Click calculate to see the tax amount and total price including tax.
how_to_read: Your result shows both the tax amount in dollars and the total final price. For businesses, this is the amount you need to collect from customers and remit to your state tax authority.
pro_tip: If you sell online across multiple states, you may have economic nexus obligations in states where you have never set foot. Research each state's sales threshold and use this calculator to estimate your tax collection requirements per state!

name: UK VAT Calculator
description: Calculate 20% or 5% VAT for UK business invoices.
what_is: Value Added Tax (VAT) is applied to most goods and services sold in the United Kingdom. The standard rate is 20%, with a reduced rate of 5% for specific categories like home energy. This calculator adds or removes VAT from any price instantly for accurate invoicing.
who_use: UK freelancers and businesses registered for VAT need this tool for every invoice they issue. It is also useful for consumers who want to understand the pre-tax cost of a purchase, or businesses checking supplier invoices.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the net price (before VAT) or the gross price (including VAT). 2. Select the applicable VAT rate — 20% standard or 5% reduced. 3. Click calculate to see the VAT amount and gross/net total.
how_to_read: If you entered the net price, the result shows how much VAT to add and the final invoice total. If you entered the gross price, it reverse-calculates the pre-tax amount — useful for reclaiming VAT on purchases.
pro_tip: You only need to register for VAT if your taxable turnover exceeds the current threshold (£90,000 as of 2024). But voluntary registration can benefit businesses that purchase a lot of VAT-liable goods, as you can reclaim that input tax!

name: Canada GST/HST Calculator
description: Estimate goods and services tax for Canadian provinces.
what_is: Canadian tax rates differ significantly by province — some charge GST only, others combine it with PST, and several provinces use a single Harmonized Sales Tax (HST). This calculator applies the correct rate for your province so every transaction is taxed accurately.
who_use: Canadian business owners, freelancers, and self-employed professionals who invoice clients need this tool. It is equally useful for consumers checking the full cost of a purchase in their province.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the pre-tax price of the product or service. 2. Input your province's combined tax rate (e.g., 13% for Ontario HST, 5% for Alberta GST only). 3. Calculate the tax amount and total price.
how_to_read: The result gives you the exact tax amount and the total cost including tax. For businesses, this is the amount to include on your invoice and remit to the CRA on your next filing period.
pro_tip: If you are a small supplier with under $30,000 CAD in annual revenue, you are not required to collect GST/HST. But once you cross that threshold, registration is mandatory. Track your revenue carefully and use this calculator to prepare!

name: US Payroll Tax Estimator
description: Estimate employer-side payroll taxes for employees.
what_is: Hiring an employee costs more than just their salary — employers are responsible for matching Social Security and Medicare taxes, paying federal and state unemployment taxes, and more. This estimator breaks down the full employer-side payroll tax burden so there are no budget surprises.
who_use: US small business owners hiring their first employees, HR managers budgeting for new hires, and entrepreneurs evaluating whether they can afford to bring on staff will all benefit from running these numbers before making an offer.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the employee's gross annual salary. 2. Input the number of pay periods per year (26 for bi-weekly, 12 for monthly). 3. Calculate to see the estimated employer tax cost per pay period and annually.
how_to_read: Your result shows the additional payroll tax cost on top of the base salary. As a rule of thumb, total employment costs run 20–30% above the gross salary figure — this tool gives you the precise number for your situation.
pro_tip: Consider offering a mix of salary and benefits (health insurance, retirement contributions) rather than a higher base salary. Benefits can be more tax-efficient for both employer and employee. Use this calculator to model the true cost difference!

name: Freelance Tax Estimator
description: Estimate self-employment taxes for 1099 gig workers.
what_is: Freelancers and independent contractors face a unique tax reality — they must pay both the employee and employer portions of Social Security and Medicare, on top of regular income tax. This estimator helps you calculate your approximate total tax bill so you can save the right amount throughout the year.
who_use: Anyone earning income on a 1099 form — including freelancers, consultants, gig workers, and self-employed professionals — needs to estimate their quarterly tax liability to avoid underpayment penalties from the IRS.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total gross freelance income for the year. 2. Input your total deductible business expenses. 3. Enter your estimated federal tax rate bracket. 4. Calculate your estimated tax liability.
how_to_read: Your result shows estimated self-employment tax and income tax combined. The net income after taxes is what you actually keep. If that number surprises you, adjust your rate card or increase deductible expense tracking.
pro_tip: Set aside 25–30% of every payment you receive into a separate tax savings account. Pay estimated taxes quarterly (April, June, September, January) to avoid IRS penalties. Use this calculator at the start of each year to set your savings rate!

name: PayPal Fee Calculator
description: Calculate fees for international and domestic payments.
what_is: PayPal charges a percentage-based fee plus a fixed transaction fee on every payment processed. These fees vary by transaction type, account tier, and whether the payment is domestic or international. This calculator shows you exactly what you will receive after PayPal takes its cut.
who_use: Freelancers invoicing clients through PayPal, online sellers accepting PayPal checkout, and anyone receiving international transfers who wants to know the actual net amount they will receive will use this regularly.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the payment amount you are expecting to receive. 2. Input the applicable PayPal fee percentage. 3. Enter the fixed fee per transaction. 4. Click calculate to see your net amount after fees.
how_to_read: The result shows the total fee deducted and the net amount deposited to your account. If you need to receive a specific amount, use this result to gross up your invoice — charge more to account for the fee.
pro_tip: To receive a specific net amount, divide your target by (1 minus the fee rate) and subtract the fixed fee. Or simply add approximately 4–5% to your invoice total to recover PayPal fees without shortchanging yourself on every transaction!

name: Stripe Fee Calculator
description: Estimate net revenue after Stripe processing fees.
what_is: Stripe is the payment infrastructure of choice for thousands of online businesses, but its processing fees directly impact your bottom line on every transaction. This calculator shows exactly how much revenue you retain after Stripe's fees so you can price your products and subscriptions accurately.
who_use: SaaS founders, subscription box companies, online course creators, and e-commerce stores using Stripe as their payment gateway need to bake processing costs into their pricing and margin models.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the transaction or subscription amount. 2. Input Stripe's fee rate (typically 2.9% for standard US cards). 3. Enter the fixed fee per transaction ($0.30 standard). 4. Calculate your net revenue.
how_to_read: Your result shows the exact Stripe fee and your net payout. For high-volume businesses, even small fee differences matter significantly at scale. Use this to model the true revenue impact of different pricing tiers.
pro_tip: Stripe offers custom pricing for businesses processing over $80,000 per month. If your volume qualifies, negotiating even 0.2% off the rate can save thousands annually. Calculate your current annual fee to know exactly what negotiating leverage you have!

name: Invoice Discount Calculator
description: Calculate net total after applying business discounts.
what_is: Offering a discount to a client should be a deliberate, calculated decision — not a gesture that quietly erodes your profitability. This calculator computes the discounted subtotal, applicable tax, and final invoice amount so every discount you offer is transparent and properly documented.
who_use: Freelancers and agencies offering volume or loyalty discounts, businesses running promotional pricing, and accountants preparing client invoices who need to calculate and verify discount amounts correctly.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your invoice subtotal before discount. 2. Input the discount percentage you are applying. 3. Enter the applicable tax rate. 4. Calculate to see the discounted total, tax amount, and final invoice figure.
how_to_read: Your result shows the step-by-step breakdown from subtotal to final amount. This transparency is important for client trust and your own records. Keep track of total discounts given each month to measure their impact on revenue.
pro_tip: Before offering any discount, calculate how many additional units or hours you need to sell to make up the lost revenue. A 10% discount requires an 11% increase in volume just to break even — this calculator keeps you financially honest!

name: Straight-Line Depreciation Calculator
description: Calculate annual asset depreciation for tax purposes.
what_is: Straight-line depreciation spreads the cost of a business asset evenly across its useful life, reducing your taxable income each year. This is the most commonly used depreciation method for accounting and tax purposes, and this calculator makes the computation instant and accurate.
who_use: Business owners who have purchased equipment, vehicles, or property for business use, accountants preparing annual financial statements, and tax filers claiming depreciation deductions will use this tool each reporting period.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the original purchase cost of the asset. 2. Input the estimated salvage value at the end of its useful life. 3. Enter the asset's useful life in years. 4. Calculate to see the annual depreciation expense.
how_to_read: Your result is the fixed annual depreciation expense you can record on your financial statements and potentially deduct on your tax return. Multiply this by the number of years held to get the total accumulated depreciation.
pro_tip: Keep a depreciation schedule for every business asset you own — equipment, computers, vehicles, and furniture all qualify. Even small assets add up to meaningful deductions. Use this calculator to build that schedule and reduce your tax bill legally!

name: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) Calculator
description: Calculate the cost to acquire a single new customer.
what_is: Customer Acquisition Cost is the total amount you spend on sales and marketing divided by the number of new customers you gain. It is one of the most important metrics for any growth-focused business, revealing whether your customer acquisition strategy is efficient or unsustainable.
who_use: Founders evaluating growth channels, CMOs optimizing marketing budgets, and investors assessing a startup's unit economics all need CAC as a foundational benchmark. It becomes most powerful when compared against Customer Lifetime Value.
how_to_use: 1. Add up all marketing and sales expenses for a given period (ads, salaries, tools, events). 2. Enter the total number of new customers acquired in that same period. 3. Click calculate to get your CAC.
how_to_read: A lower CAC is better — but context matters. A $200 CAC is excellent if a customer generates $2,000 in lifetime value, but disastrous if they only spend $150 total. Always evaluate CAC alongside CLV for a complete picture.
pro_tip: Calculate CAC separately per channel — Google Ads, social media, referrals, content. You will almost always find one channel acquiring customers at a fraction of the cost. Double down there. Use this calculator to run the numbers after every campaign!

name: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Calculator
description: Estimate total revenue per customer over their lifetime.
what_is: Customer Lifetime Value is the total revenue you can expect from a single customer throughout their entire relationship with your business. Understanding CLV tells you how much you can afford to spend acquiring each new customer and which customer segments are most valuable to retain.
who_use: E-commerce businesses, SaaS companies, subscription services, and any business with repeat customers should calculate CLV regularly. It is the essential counterpart to CAC for evaluating whether your business model is sustainable.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the average value of a single purchase. 2. Input how many times a typical customer buys from you per year. 3. Enter how many years a customer typically stays with your business. 4. Calculate your CLV.
how_to_read: Your CLV result is the total expected revenue per customer. As a healthy benchmark, your CLV should be at least 3 times your Customer Acquisition Cost. If it is lower, you are spending too much to acquire customers relative to what they generate.
pro_tip: Increasing customer lifespan by even 6 months can dramatically improve CLV without any additional acquisition spend. Focus on onboarding, engagement, and loyalty programs — retention is often more profitable than acquisition. Calculate the difference here!

name: Marketing Expense Ratio Calculator
description: Track your marketing spend relative to total revenue.
what_is: The marketing expense ratio shows what percentage of your total revenue you are reinvesting into marketing and advertising. It is the guardrail metric that helps you determine whether your marketing investment is proportionate to the revenue it generates.
who_use: CMOs justifying marketing budgets to boards, business owners evaluating whether they are over- or under-investing in growth, and financial analysts benchmarking spend efficiency across business units all rely on this ratio.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your total marketing and advertising spend for the period. 2. Input your total revenue for the same period. 3. Click calculate to get your marketing expense ratio as a percentage.
how_to_read: Most B2B companies spend 5–10% of revenue on marketing; B2C companies often spend 10–20%. High-growth startups may invest 25%+ to acquire market share early. Compare your ratio to industry benchmarks — not just competitors.
pro_tip: If your marketing ratio is rising but revenue growth is not keeping pace, your campaigns are becoming less efficient. Use this calculator quarterly to spot that trend early and reallocate budget toward higher-performing channels before the gap widens!

name: Customer Retention Rate Calculator
description: Measure the percentage of customers who stay loyal.
what_is: Customer retention rate measures what percentage of your existing customers you successfully kept during a given period. It is one of the most powerful indicators of product-market fit, service quality, and long-term business health — because retaining a customer is almost always cheaper than replacing one.
who_use: Subscription businesses, SaaS companies, membership organizations, and any business with recurring revenue relationships should track retention rate monthly. It is a leading indicator of future revenue stability.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the number of customers at the start of the period. 2. Input how many new customers you acquired during the period. 3. Enter the total customers at the end of the period. 4. Calculate your retention rate.
how_to_read: A retention rate above 85% is strong for most industries. SaaS businesses often target 90%+. Below 70% signals serious product or service issues that require immediate attention before the business loses its customer base faster than it can replace it.
pro_tip: Even a 5% improvement in retention can increase profits by 25–95% according to classic customer loyalty research. Use this calculator monthly, set a target retention rate, and track the impact of every service improvement you make!

name: Email Open Rate ROI Calculator
description: Measure your email marketing campaign performance and return.
what_is: Email marketing consistently delivers one of the highest ROIs of any digital channel — but only if you are tracking performance correctly. This calculator measures your open rate and revenue per email sent, giving you the data you need to optimize every future campaign.
who_use: Email marketers, digital agencies, e-commerce businesses using automated email flows, and founders running newsletter businesses all need to quantify their email performance beyond vanity metrics like subscriber count.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the total number of emails sent in your campaign. 2. Input the total number of emails opened. 3. Enter the total revenue generated from this campaign. 4. Calculate your open rate and revenue per email.
how_to_read: An average open rate of 20–30% is healthy across most industries. Revenue per email sent is the more important metric — even a low open rate campaign can be highly profitable if the segment is well-targeted and the offer is strong.
pro_tip: Subject line testing (A/B split) is the single highest-leverage email optimization. A 5% improvement in open rate on a list of 10,000 means 500 more people see your message every send. Use this calculator to quantify what that improvement is worth in revenue!

name: Hourly to Salary Calculator
description: Convert your hourly wage into an equivalent yearly salary.
what_is: Comparing hourly contract rates to salaried job offers is surprisingly tricky when benefits, vacation time, and taxes enter the picture. This calculator converts your hourly rate into an annual equivalent so you can make direct, apples-to-apples comparisons between compensation packages.
who_use: Freelancers evaluating whether to take a full-time role, part-time workers understanding their annual earning potential, HR managers pricing contract vs. permanent roles, and job seekers negotiating between offers will all benefit from this conversion.
how_to_use: 1. Enter your hourly pay rate. 2. Input the number of hours you work per week. 3. Enter the number of weeks you work per year (52 for full-year, less for contractors). 4. Calculate your equivalent annual salary.
how_to_read: Your result is the gross annual equivalent of your hourly rate. Remember that salaried positions often include health insurance, retirement contributions, and paid leave that can add 20–30% to the value of the compensation package.
pro_tip: As a freelancer, your hourly rate needs to be significantly higher than an equivalent salaried role to account for self-employment tax, unpaid time between projects, and benefits you fund yourself. Use this calculator to find the minimum rate you should accept!

name: Employee Turnover Rate Calculator
description: Calculate the rate at which employees leave your company.
what_is: Employee turnover rate measures what percentage of your workforce leaves and needs to be replaced during a given period. High turnover is expensive — recruiting, hiring, and onboarding a replacement can cost 50–200% of a departing employee's annual salary. This calculator shows you your exact rate so you can act on it.
who_use: HR managers building workforce strategies, business owners trying to understand culture and retention challenges, and executives benchmarking their people metrics against industry standards will use this calculation in every quarterly review.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the number of employees who left during the period. 2. Input the average number of employees during that same period (start + end divided by 2). 3. Calculate your annualized turnover rate.
how_to_read: An annual turnover rate below 10% is considered low and healthy. 10–20% is moderate. Above 20% signals a systemic retention problem — and above 30% indicates a crisis that is actively harming business performance and culture.
pro_tip: Track turnover by department and tenure — not just company-wide. Losing employees in their first 90 days points to onboarding failures, while losing 3-year veterans suggests compensation or career growth gaps. Use this calculator to identify the patterns!

name: EBITDA Margin Calculator
description: Professional earnings analysis for businesses and investors.
what_is: EBITDA — Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization — strips away financing and accounting decisions to reveal the raw operational profitability of a business. This calculator gives you the EBITDA margin, enabling meaningful comparisons across companies and industries.
who_use: Business owners preparing for investment or acquisition conversations, investors comparing profitability across companies with different capital structures, and financial analysts presenting business performance to stakeholders all need EBITDA margin.
how_to_use: 1. Enter the company's net income. 2. Add back interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization expenses. 3. Input total revenue. 4. Calculate your EBITDA and EBITDA margin percentage.
how_to_read: An EBITDA margin above 15% is generally healthy. Private equity buyers typically look for businesses with stable margins above 20%. If your EBITDA margin is lower than your industry benchmark, dig into which cost category is out of line.
pro_tip: EBITDA is the starting point for most acquisition multiples — businesses are often valued at 4–8x EBITDA. Improving your EBITDA margin by just 2% can add significantly more to your business valuation than the margin improvement itself suggests. Calculate yours now!

name: Current Ratio Analysis Calculator
description: Evaluate your business's short-term financial strength.
what_is: The current ratio is a fundamental liquidity metric that compares what a business owns in the short term against what it owes in the short term. It tells lenders, investors, and business owners whether the company can meet its obligations over the next 12 months without needing emergency financing.
who_use: Business owners preparing for a bank loan application, accountants conducting annual financial reviews, and investors screening companies for financial stability all use the current ratio as a first-line solvency check.
how_to_use: 1. Add up all current assets (cash, receivables, inventory, prepaid expenses). 2. Add up all current liabilities (payables, short-term debt, accrued expenses). 3. Click calculate to get your current ratio.
how_to_read: A ratio of 2.0 means you have twice as many current assets as current liabilities — comfortable. A ratio of 1.0–1.5 is workable but tight. Below 1.0 means you technically cannot cover your short-term obligations — a red flag for any lender.
pro_tip: If your current ratio is low, speed up accounts receivable collection and slow down payments to suppliers where possible. Even small shifts in cash timing can move your ratio significantly. Track this every quarter alongside the Working Capital Ratio for a complete liquidity picture!
